secrets.name: Secrets
secrets.description: Hardcoded API keys, tokens, database credentials, and other secrets in source code or system prompts.
secrets.rationale: Leaked secrets in git history can never be unleaked. One commit ruins the rotation cost forever — every collaborator and every CI run since the commit has seen the key, and every fork carries a copy.

prompt-injection.name: Prompt injection
prompt-injection.description: Code patterns that let user-controlled strings rewrite the model's behaviour by sneaking into the system prompt without separation.
prompt-injection.rationale: The textbook attack surface for any LLM app. If untrusted input lands raw inside the prompt, a single 'Ignore previous instructions' message pivots the agent into the attacker's hands.

system-prompt-leakage.name: System prompt leakage
system-prompt-leakage.description: System prompts containing credentials, internal URLs, business logic, or PII that should not be reachable through the model.
system-prompt-leakage.rationale: System prompts are easy to extract with one-line attacks. Anything inside — secrets, internal URLs, business rules — is effectively public the moment the agent goes live.

tool-design.name: Tool design
tool-design.description: Tool definitions missing descriptions or parameter schemas, accepting raw SQL/shell/paths, or carrying destructive verbs without an approval gate.
tool-design.rationale: Models invoke tools confidently based on their description. A loose or destructive tool definition is a confused-deputy ready to fire — at scale, with no human in the loop.

output-handling.name: Output handling
output-handling.description: LLM output flowing into eval, file paths, fetch URLs, SQL, or innerHTML without sanitisation — the model's reply becomes attacker-controlled when prompt injection lands.
output-handling.rationale: Model output is attacker-controlled through prompt injection. Anything you would never do to user input — eval it, concatenate it into SQL, render it as HTML — never do to LLM output either.

supply-chain.name: Supply chain
supply-chain.description: Unsafe deserialisation of model weights (pickle, torch.load) and typosquatted LLM-related dependencies that exfiltrate keys at install time.
supply-chain.rationale: Loading a pickled model file from an untrusted source is equivalent to running its author's script. Typosquat npm/PyPI packages exfiltrate API keys via postinstall before any of your code runs.

unbounded-consumption.name: Unbounded consumption
unbounded-consumption.description: LLM API calls without an output-token cap, recursive prompts, or token-flood patterns that let one user drain a whole team's budget.
unbounded-consumption.rationale: An attacker only needs one input that forces an arbitrarily long output to drain the entire shared token budget. Cost-DoS is cheap for the attacker and expensive for the team.

dynamic-self-attack.name: Dynamic self-attack
dynamic-self-attack.description: Live testing of the deployed AI agent: Audithex sends crafted attack prompts to the agent's HTTPS endpoint and grades the responses. Disabled by default — opt-in because each attack consumes paid tokens.
dynamic-self-attack.rationale: Static analysis finds code that *might* be exploitable. Dynamic testing finds whether the deployed system *actually is*. Both are necessary; neither replaces the other.
